# Questions in Livestream 237

Live Stream #237: Tea Spilled All Over The Floor

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=445" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:25</a> | Was Peter the first pope, and is there evidence he served as bishop of Rome? | He says the evidence is exceptionally thin and that Paul is far better attested in Rome than Peter. | He argues that early sources do not clearly place Peter in Rome, while they do place Peter in Jerusalem and Asia Minor. By contrast, Paul is strongly attested in Rome through his appeal to Caesar, his journey there, and his stay there. The later documents used to build a Petrine succession are described as late, contested, and dependent on weak sources. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=839" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:59</a> | What does "temples" in Romans 2:22 refer to? | He says it refers to literal pagan temples and condemns hypocritically robbing them. | He reads Romans 2:22 as a charge of hypocrisy: someone who says he hates idols should not enrich himself by stealing from idol temples. The point is not that pagan temples are good, but that Christians should not repay evil with evil, loot temples, burn them, or claim to advance Christ by destructive behavior. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=1069" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:49</a> | Did the ancient Near Eastern view of a god's power depend on how much worship it received? | He says no; offerings mattered, but powerful gods could exist without temples or worshippers. | He rejects the "American Gods" model for the ancient Near East. Gods could need food, drink, clothing, and cult maintenance, but their power was not proportional to worshippers. He gives Geb as an example of a powerful Egyptian deity with no real temple cult or worshippers, yet still regarded as powerful because of his place in divine procreation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=1381" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:01</a> | Have you made it through the "He Who Must Be Named" educational presentation yet? | Not yet. He says he is about two-thirds through it and still plans to finish it. | He says he has not forgotten the requested review and is about two-thirds of the way through the presentation. ETS-related material and other requested reviews have crowded the schedule, but he still intends to finish it after handling the newer "spilled tea" video. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=1466" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:26</a> | What did you think of David Wood's ETS Quran lecture? | He says it initially sounded promising but was badly undermined in the question period. | He says he attended the paper and, as a non-specialist in Quranic or Islamic literature, initially thought it sounded good. The lecture was then severely challenged during the question period, which he frames as normal for academic conferences where new ideas are presented so other scholars can test them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=1886" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:26</a> | What specifically did Wood get wrong according to Ibrahim? | He says Ibrahim argued Wood's methodological distinction collapsed because tafsir used the same isnad logic Wood reserved for hadith. | He summarizes Ibrahim's criticism as methodological: Wood tried to distinguish hadith evidence from tafsir evidence, but Ibrahim argued that both used the same chain-of-transmission method. If the method is materially the same, then Wood's central thesis lacks warrant and the argument falls. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=1965" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:45</a> | Could major errors still appear even after peer review? | Yes. He says that is why scholars present work widely before publication. | He says peer review can still miss errors, but the point is to catch major problems earlier if possible. Presenting at conferences lets other scholars "kick the tires," ask whether the argument follows the evidence, and expose weaknesses before publication. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=2003" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:23</a> | Should Wood have avoided posting that lecture on YouTube before revising it? | He says yes, because the core substance still needed more work after the criticism it received. | He says he would not have posted the lecture as-is because the criticism amounted, in his view, to a substantive retraction or at least a demand for major revision. Since the core argument had been gutted in the discussion, publishing only the paper without the critical exchange was a bad academic decision. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=2209" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:49</a> | How does having a philosophy degree affect doing research in another field? | He says cross-field work is common, but it requires extra ramp-up and harder work to be publishable. | He says many scholars work outside their narrow degree field, but they have to do the extra training and homework needed for the new area. A philosophy background may help with argumentation, but it does not remove the need to master the evidence, methods, and literature of the field being addressed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=2399" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:59</a> | Did Ibrahim attack only one part of Wood's presentation, or the Satanic Verses narrative more broadly? | He says Ibrahim mainly attacked Wood's methodology, not the broader question of whether early Islamic scholars treated the narrative as genuine. | He distinguishes the methodological critique from the larger historical claim. Ibrahim was not simply denying that early Islamic authorities treated the Satanic Verses tradition as genuine; he was attacking Wood's attempt to privilege one category of transmission evidence over another. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=2587" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:07</a> | What wine, or failing that what soft drink, pairs well with salmon? | He recommends lighter pairings such as white wine, rose or blush wine, or prosecco, and says to avoid heavy reds. | He recommends lighter drinks because salmon's flavor can be overwhelmed. White wines, rose or blush wines, and prosecco are presented as good pairings, while heavy reds such as merlot are discouraged because they would bury the nuance of the fish. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=2759" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:59</a> | What is your opinion on AI-powered glasses? | He says they are not worth the privacy tradeoffs because they would gather and transmit a great deal of personal information. | He objects mainly on privacy grounds. Glasses that see and process what the wearer sees would collect sensitive information about the user and nearby people, and he does not think the convenience is worth the amount of data that would have to be captured and transmitted. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=3159" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:39</a> | Do denominational disagreements on salvific doctrine falsify Christianity? | He says no. Disagreement over interpretation does not disprove Christianity itself. | He treats denominational disagreement as an interpretive and ecclesial problem, not as a logical refutation of Christianity. Christians can disagree over how salvation-related doctrines are formulated while still sharing the basic claim that Christianity is true. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=3505" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:25</a> | What is your view of global warming? | He says warming is real but treats climate change as complex, with shifts that can create both winners and losers. | He accepts that warming is happening, but he resists treating climate effects as simple or uniform. His answer emphasizes regional complexity: some changes are harmful, some may benefit certain areas, and the practical consequences depend on local conditions rather than one global slogan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=3966" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:06</a> | Has anyone requested you to "popcorn" review them? | He says yes in general, but no one besides the asker has asked him to "popcorn" that specific person. | He says he receives multiple requests for review-style responses and regards several of them as legitimate candidates. For the specific person named by the asker, however, he says only that asker had requested it so far. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=4090" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:10</a> | Is Pharaoh personally responsible in Exodus 4:21 if God hardened his heart, and why would God do that? | He says Pharaoh first hardens his own heart, so he remains responsible, and God then confirms that path to display his glory. | He walks through the Exodus sequence and argues that Pharaoh repeatedly hardens his own heart before the text says the Lord hardens it. On that reading, God is not forcing an innocent Pharaoh into rebellion but confirming Pharaoh's chosen opposition so the plagues can display God's power and expose Pharaoh's powerlessness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=4655" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:35</a> | Follow-up: did the Lord harden Pharaoh's heart only after Pharaoh had already hardened his own? | He says yes, that is how he understands the sequence. | He reiterates that Pharaoh had the choice first and chose resistance. God's later hardening is understood as giving Pharaoh over to the path he had already taken, so Pharaoh's responsibility remains intact. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=4919" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:59</a> | Is the Society of Biblical Literature still a Christian institution? | He says no. In his view it has not been a Christian institution for years. | He distinguishes SBL from confessionally Christian academic bodies. In his view, SBL may include Christian scholars and biblical studies work, but institutionally it has not functioned as a Christian organization for a long time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=5073" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:33</a> | How are ancient Near Eastern primary-source documents such as the Mari letters archived and studied? | He says they are typically held by museums or universities, carefully conserved, and preserved further through publication. | He explains that tablets and documents are usually curated in institutional collections, not simply handled casually by individual scholars. They are conserved, cataloged, copied, photographed or transcribed, and then made usable to the wider field through publications and reference editions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=5742" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:42</a> | Did the ancient Egyptians deface disliked pharaohs' statues specifically by removing the nose? | He says a missing nose alone is not enough to prove deliberate defacement because noses are the most fragile part of a statue. | He warns against overreading broken noses. Deliberate defacement did happen, but noses protrude and break easily, so a missing nose by itself is weak evidence. Stronger cases require broader damage patterns, inscriptions, or context showing intentional erasure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=6111" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:51</a> | Can we say for sure that Ushu on Sennacherib's wall is Iskenderun before Alexander? | He says he has not read the article and is not the best Assyriology source to answer that question. | He declines to give a firm answer because he has not read the relevant article and the question depends on Assyriological identification work. He points the asker toward better Assyriology expertise rather than pretending confidence on a source he has not evaluated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=6261" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:21</a> | Could the Genesis 3 serpent originally have been intended as a lion because of claims about proto-Semitic n-h-sh? | He says no. The Genesis 3 imagery points to a snake, not a lion. | He rejects the proposal because the narrative imagery in Genesis 3 fits a serpent: the creature crawls, is associated with dust, and is identified by the serpent language in the text. A proposed proto-Semitic connection is not enough to overturn the literary and contextual indicators. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=6905" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:05</a> | Did you present at ETS this year? | No. He says this was a writing year for him, though he hopes to present next year. | He says he did not present at ETS because this year was focused on writing rather than conference papers. He still attended and hopes to present again the following year if his work schedule allows. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=7112" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:32</a> | Are you a member of EPS? | He says no, because he does not meet EPS's philosophy-field credential requirement. | He explains that EPS membership is tied to credentials in philosophy or a closely related field. Since his academic training is not in philosophy, he says he does not qualify even though he interacts with philosophical topics. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=7332" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:12</a> | Do you think the god Min was connected to Narmer? | He says no; he treats the Min cult as much later and not related. | He does not see a connection between Min and Narmer. His answer treats the Min cult evidence as later than Narmer and therefore not a good basis for linking the two. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=7395" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:15</a> | What mind-altering substances have you experimented with, and has meditation or self-dissolution affected your spiritual growth? | He says none recreationally and rejects that path as unnecessary and spiritually risky. | He says he has not used recreational mind-altering substances and does not see self-dissolution practices as a necessary path to spiritual growth. His concern is that pursuing altered states can become spiritually dangerous rather than spiritually clarifying. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=7728" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:48</a> | Does James Hoffmeier attend ETS? | He says no, not usually; he sees Hoffmeier at ASOR instead. | He says Hoffmeier is not usually someone he encounters at ETS. The conference where he tends to see him is ASOR, which better matches Hoffmeier's field and professional circuit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=7881" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:21</a> | Has Kenneth Kitchen ever gone to either of those conferences? | He says Kitchen attended a few times long ago, but they were never really his main conference circuit. | He says Kitchen did attend some of those conferences in the past, but not as his regular professional home. Kitchen's main academic circles were elsewhere, so appearances at ETS or similar meetings were occasional rather than typical. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=7932" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:12</a> | Isn't William Dever a minimalist? | He says yes, though not as rigidly as before. | He identifies Dever as a minimalist, but says Dever's position has softened compared with earlier forms of minimalism. The answer places Dever within that camp while noting that the old maximalist-minimalist divide has become less rigid. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=8343" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:03</a> | Could it be that some people think Ron Wyatt was normal? | He says some people treat Wyatt as a prophetically inspired figure, but he views the continuing movement as a grift sustained by Wyatt's followers and museum. | He says Wyatt's supporters often treat him as more than an ordinary amateur, even as a figure with prophetic or special insight. He rejects that framing and portrays the continuing Wyatt movement, including its museum and followers, as a money-making enterprise built around bad claims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=8617" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:37</a> | Do you know of cases where a biblical author used the wrong Greek or Hebrew word but context still made the meaning clear, and were their word choices infallible? | He says he does not see clear cases of outright wrong word choice, but he places infallibility more on the message than on translation-level wording. | He distinguishes lexical wording from the communicated message. He does not offer clear examples of biblical authors choosing the wrong word, and he frames infallibility as applying to what Scripture communicates rather than to a wooden theory of every possible translation-level wording choice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=9203" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:23</a> | Do all minimalists refute the United Monarchy, and do all maximalists read Kings and Chronicles literally? | He says no to both. In his view the two camps have moved closer to the middle over time. | He says the labels are no longer as absolute as they are often made to sound. Minimalists need not deny every form of a United Monarchy, and maximalists need not read Kings and Chronicles with wooden literalism. He sees both camps as having moderated toward more nuanced middle positions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=9278" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:38</a> | Is ABR pseudo-maximalism? | He says ABR is literalist in some areas, but often more willing than many maximalists to use redaction criticism. | He says ABR can be quite literalist, but the label "pseudo-maximalist" is too simple. In some areas ABR scholars are more willing than strict maximalists to use redaction criticism, so their position does not map neatly onto the standard maximalist category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=9501" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:38:21</a> | Are there any topics or added categories that should go into a questionnaire for interviewing church leaders? | He recommends adding biblical anthropology to the existing categories. | He suggests biblical anthropology as an important added category because views of humanity shape many downstream doctrines and pastoral decisions. The answer implies that a church-leader questionnaire should test not only standard doctrinal categories but also how leaders understand human nature. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=9603" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:03</a> | Who are most likely Gog and Magog today? | He leans toward identifying them with Asia Minor or Turkey, while noting uncertainty. | He says the identification is uncertain, but he leans toward Asia Minor or Turkey rather than a confident modern geopolitical target. He treats Gog and Magog as difficult prophetic referents and presents the answer as a cautious likelihood, not a settled claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=9713" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:53</a> | Why is the Spirit of God grammatically feminine in the Old Testament but referred to as he in the New Testament? | He says Hebrew `ruach` is grammatically feminine, which is a language feature rather than a claim about gender, while New Testament pronouns give the personal reference. | He explains that grammatical gender does not equal biological or personal gender. Hebrew `ruach` being feminine reflects Hebrew grammar, while the New Testament's masculine personal reference reflects how the Spirit is spoken of personally in that linguistic and theological context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=10094" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:14</a> | What is the difference between schematic or thematic numbers and idealized numbers in 1 Kings 6? | He treats the 480 years as an idiomatic or symbolic number rather than a straightforward literal count, unlike the regnal-year reference tied to Solomon. | He distinguishes the chronological anchor in Solomon's reign from the larger 480-year figure. The regnal-year reference is treated as ordinary dating, while 480 functions as an idealized or schematic number, likely organized around symbolic units rather than a precise arithmetic total. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=10924" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:04</a> | How does ABR read the parenthetical note in Numbers 13:22? | He says it depends on the scholar; some would defend the Masoretic wording more strictly, while others would treat it as a scribal gloss. | He says there is not one ABR answer. Some ABR-associated scholars are strong Masoretic Text maximalists and would defend the wording as it stands, while others may prefer a Septuagint reading or identify the note as a scribal gloss. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=11063" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:23</a> | Do you have a hypothesis for what happened in 1 Samuel 13:1? | He thinks the text became damaged very early and later copies and translations are compensating for a fragmentary reading. | He says the Masoretic text of 1 Samuel 13:1 is fragmentary, with missing or difficult numbers for Saul's age and reign. His hypothesis is that the text was damaged in antiquity and later copyists or translators supplied numbers from other evidence or idiomatic conventions to make sense of it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=11590" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:10</a> | Do we have evidence that Egyptians used intelligence tactics or paid informants against enemies or neighbors? | He says yes. Egypt used spies, paid for information, and employed counterintelligence. | He says Egyptian sources do show intelligence work: spies, paid information, informants, and counterintelligence. He mentions a case where a general paid for information, discovered the informant was false, and executed the false informant at the city gate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=11765" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:05</a> | Are any parts of the Apocrypha inspired, even if some historical material in them is useful? | He says no. Some parts may be useful or wise, but he does not treat any of them as inspired Scripture. | He says the Apocrypha failed the Old Testament canonicity process associated with Ezra and Nehemiah, including concerns such as prophecy, historical reliability, and pseudepigraphy. He allows that the books can contain wisdom or useful historical material, but usefulness does not make them inspired. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=11910" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:30</a> | Why did Jude quote Enoch, and why did Paul quote Menander? | He says biblical writers can quote non-canonical sources selectively to make a point without endorsing them as inspired. | He says biblical authors are not limited to quoting inspired books. They can cite noncanonical writers, aphorisms, or traditions to make a point, just as later Christian writers quote pagan philosophers without canonizing them. Jude's use of Enoch therefore supports an argument without making Enoch Scripture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=12526" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:46</a> | What is the Milo in Jerusalem? | He says he had not looked it up yet and could not answer on the spot. | He declines to answer because he had not yet checked the relevant Jerusalem map or reference material. The transcript supports only a limited answer: he did not know at the moment and would need to look it up before giving a responsible response. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gCl6LNO1C9k?t=12597" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:57</a> | Why did you get the Ge'ez book? | He says it interested him as one more Semitic language he did not know. | He says the book appealed to him because Ge'ez was another Semitic language he did not know. It looked interesting, so he picked it up out of linguistic curiosity. |
